seo.title: Dr. Leiza Hollas — Cardiovascular Surgeon
seo.description: Dr. Leiza Loiane Hollas, cardiovascular surgeon, member of SBCCV. Specialized in cardiac surgery, valve surgery, and surgical simulation. Based in Curitiba, PR.

sobre.altImagem: Photo of Dr. Leiza Loiane Hollas
sobre.texto:
Cardiovascular surgeon and member of the Brazilian Society of Cardiovascular Surgery (SBCCV), she focuses her practice on the surgical treatment of heart diseases, prioritizing safety, technical precision, and individualized care.

She completed her medical residency in Cardiovascular Surgery at the Brazilian Red Cross Hospital – Paraná (2024), consolidating specialized training in high-complexity procedures and integrated cardiovascular patient management.

Graduated in Medicine from the Pontifical Catholic University of Paraná (2018), her practice is mainly focused on cardiovascular surgery, valve surgery, and surgical simulation, constantly seeking to combine innovation, scientific evidence, and excellence in patient care.

In addition to her clinical and surgical work, she maintains continuous scientific activity, with articles published in national and international journals. She is a member of The Valve Club, an initiative dedicated to disseminating medical knowledge through the discussion of studies related to valvular heart disease and structural heart disease — fields that are constantly evolving in modern cardiology.

She believes that cardiovascular surgery requires not only technical mastery but also attentive listening and responsibility in every decision. For this reason, her work is guided by building a relationship of trust with each patient, providing individualized care throughout every stage of treatment.

card1.texto: Maintain absolute fasting for 8 hours before surgery, including water.

card2.texto: Inform your doctor about all medications used and known allergies.

card3.texto: Bring a photo ID, recent tests, and insurance authorization if applicable.

card4.texto: Take a shower with antiseptic soap the night before and the day of surgery.

card1.texto: Confirm your full name before any exams or procedures. Keep the ID bracelet until discharge.

card2.texto: Do not provide data or make payments over the phone. Contact the hospital's cashier for questions.

card1.texto: Even if you feel fine, call the nursing team before getting up.

card2.texto: Keep personal items within reach and the call button accessible.

card1.texto: After using the bathroom, before meals, and when entering or leaving the hospital. Use 70% alcohol if not visibly dirty.

card2.texto: Ask companions and visitors to clean their hands frequently too.

card1.texto: AAS, Clopidogrel, Marevan, Marcoumar, Eliquis must be suspended 5 days before surgery. Rivaroxaban, 2 days before. Always follow medical advice!

card2.texto: Check in the day before surgery at 2 PM. Bring documents, tests, authorization, and personal items.

card3.texto: Remove false eyelashes, gel nails, piercings, spacers, hair extensions, and valuables.

card4.texto: After insurance approval, schedule the pre-anesthetic evaluation in advance.

card1.texto: Yes, one companion over 18 is allowed during hospitalization.

card2.texto: Bringing outside food is not allowed without permission from the nutrition team.

card3.texto: Visits are allowed from 2 PM to 8 PM. Under 16s are not allowed in ICUs.

card4.titulo: How to speak with the medical team?
card4.texto: Medical information is given by the responsible doctor to the legal guardian.

theValveClub.descricao: The Valve Club platform aims to disseminate knowledge and science through articles related to valvular heart disease and structural heart disease — a growing area of interest in cardiology, as well as a subject that sparks curiosity in those who interact with patients affected by these conditions.

livro.titulo: Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery from LACTCV
livro.resumo: The LACTCV Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery introduces essential fundamentals of the specialty—from anatomy to the most common conditions—through a clear and accessible didactic approach.
livro.conteudo:
The LACTCV Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery was developed to introduce readers to the essential fundamentals of thoracic and cardiovascular surgery, covering topics from anatomical concepts to the most frequent pathologies in the specialty.

The purpose of this work is to provide didactic, objective, and accessible content, especially aimed at medical students and trainees, offering a solid foundation for understanding the field's core principles.

Throughout its pages, readers are guided through a learning journey that brings together technical knowledge, conceptual clarity, and practical application, serving as a starting point for continuous development in a specialty that demands precision, dedication, and constant growth.

This book is an invitation to study and to build a professional path grounded in excellence and commitment to healthcare.

artigos.subtitulo: Is every procedure a little genius and a little madness?
artigos.resumo: The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population. Today there is great medical interest in seeking interventional strategies — both conventional surgical and minimally invasive (transcatheter).
artigos.conteudo:
The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population and, given the high prevalence, there is currently great medical interest in seeking interventional strategies – both conventional surgical and minimally invasive (transcatheter) – that can be indicated for patients with valvular heart disease.

In recent years, transcatheter technologies have gained prominence in treating symptomatic mitral valve insufficiency. According to the latest Brazilian Valvular Heart Disease Guideline (2020), the percutaneous method should be reserved for patients with moderate to severe or severe mitral insufficiency, of both primary and secondary etiologies, with refractory symptoms and high or prohibitive surgical risk.

MitraClip® uses an approach similar to the Alfieri surgical procedure, created in 1991, but in a minimally invasive manner. During the procedure, the device is inserted through catheters in the femoral vein and guided to the mitral valve through a transseptal puncture.

Using data from the CUTTING-EDGE Registry, the study compared the outcome of mitral valve surgery after failure of percutaneous treatment with MitraClip® in patients with primary versus secondary MI. 330 patients were analyzed from July 2009 to July 2020. One-year mortality after surgery was 31.3% overall, being higher in patients with secondary MI (38.3% vs 23.2%).

These data show that we must seek satisfactory results after TEER, with the aim of avoiding future complications and ensuring the best quality of life for the patient. Understanding the risks involved highlights the importance of a multidisciplinary Heart Team approach.

artigos.resumo: The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population. When valve replacement is necessary, we must be attentive to the occurrence of structural degeneration of bioprostheses.
artigos.conteudo:
The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population, and given the high prevalence of rheumatic fever, with consequent involvement of young patients, the demand for valve intervention is directly impacted.

When valve replacement is necessary, especially when choosing a bioprosthesis, we must be attentive to the occurrence of its structural degeneration, a diagnosis often made late, which can lead to clinical deterioration and ventricular function impairment.

According to American College of Cardiology (ACC) guidelines, transthoracic echocardiographic control after valve replacement is suggested at 1, 3, and 12 months after the procedure, and then annually. During this follow-up, active search should be conducted for functional class changes and new symptoms that may indicate bioprosthetic dysfunction.

These dysfunctions can be classified into stages, initially with identification of valve dysfunction without hemodynamic changes, up to more advanced stages, where there is prosthetic dysfunction associated with more severe hemodynamic changes. They can also be classified into categories: structural dysfunction (calcification or rupture of leaflets) or non-structural (paraprosthetic leak, pannus, thrombi, endocarditis, and mismatch).

Of the available diagnostic exams, echocardiography (transthoracic or transesophageal) is the main ally. In cases of doubt or to improve analysis of possible aortic bioprosthetic thrombosis, computed tomography angiography can be used.

The objective of structural valve interventions is to optimize hemodynamic load, reduce symptoms, improve quality of life, and possibly prolong survival. Not every bioprosthetic dysfunction will have reintervention as final treatment, often requiring medical management and lifestyle changes.

artigos.subtitulo: A duel to tame aortic aneurysms!
artigos.resumo: In 1900, Dr. William Osler already pointed out: 'there is no disease more conducive to clinical humility than aortic aneurysms'. To this day, aortic diseases are a clinical-surgical challenge.
artigos.conteudo:
In 1900, Dr. William Osler already pointed out a medical truth: 'there is no disease more conducive to clinical humility than aortic aneurysms'. Osler's statement is true, as to this day, aortic diseases are a clinical-surgical challenge for the medical field.

It is worth remembering that, on average, the normal diameter of the ascending aorta is about 30mm and dilations above 50% of this value are already considered outside the normal pattern. There are several predisposing factors: smoking, hypertension, genetic disorders (Marfan and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome), infectious (syphilis), and congenital.

Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital alteration, present in 1 to 2% of the world population and is related to thoracic aortic aneurysms in 50% of cases. The risk of developing an aneurysm is up to 0.1% per year and corresponds to a pathology with high mortality.

It is established that patients with ascending aortic aneurysm with diameter ≥ 55mm require surgical intervention. The most practiced surgery is Bentall and De-Bono, which consists of combined replacement of the valve, aortic root, and ascending aorta. However, patients will always be subject to anticoagulation or bioprosthetic deterioration.

As a surgical alternative, there are two techniques involving the 'valve sparing' concept: the Yacoub technique, created in 1982, in which new sinuses of Valsalva are made using Dacron tube, and the David technique, idealized in 1992, in which the tube is implanted directly on the aortic valve annulus with reimplantation of the coronary artery ostia.

A recent systematic review compared the durability and safety of David and Yacoub procedures in patients with bicuspid aortic valve. 1159 patients were included (87.4% male, mean age 44.9 years). Mean mortality was 1.7% at thirty days. Kaplan-Meier survival estimate at 5, 10, and 15 years was 96%, 90%, and 87%, while the estimate of patients free from reoperation in the same period was 96%, 91%, and 88%.

Even in patients with bicuspid aortic valve, preservation of the native aortic valve proves to be clearly more advantageous over replacement, as it presents more physiological hemodynamics, relief from the need for anticoagulation, and greater durability.

artigos.resumo: Intervention in tricuspid valvopathy has clear indication when it occurs concomitantly with mitral and aortic valvopathy intervention. When present in isolation, intervention indication is still controversial.
artigos.conteudo:
Intervention in tricuspid valvopathy has clear indication when it occurs concomitantly with mitral and aortic valvopathy intervention. When present in isolation, the indication for intervention is still controversial among surgeons and cardiologists, with more prevalence in the presence of valvular stenosis.

It is common knowledge that tricuspid valve diseases lead to hemodynamic changes in the cardiac cycle that, depending on their severity, can lead to right heart failure, a severe clinical picture with high morbidity and mortality.

When compared to isolated surgical treatment of aortic and mitral valves, isolated tricuspid valve intervention presents operative mortality 2 to 3 times higher, around 8 to 9%.

A recent meta-analysis with 15,069 patients undergoing isolated tricuspid valve surgery compared valve repair with valve replacement. Results favored valve repair regarding postoperative outcomes, such as atrioventricular blocks, need for permanent pacemaker implantation, mortality, and lower incidence of renal insufficiency.

Therefore, knowing the pathophysiology of tricuspid valvopathy and the correct indication for transcatheter or surgical interventions remain topics of broad debate and importance. Only then will the nickname 'forgotten valve' be resigned to the past.
